draft.introduction: The Rasa'il Ikhwan al-Safa ("Epistles of the Brethren of Purity") is an anonymous 10th-century encyclopedia of 52 epistles covering mathematics, science, and theology. Epistle 1 treats arithmetic as the foundation of all knowledge.

translation.text: Know, O brother—may God aid you and us with a spirit from Him—that the science of number is the root of all the sciences, the foundation of wisdom, the source of knowledge, and the pillar of meaning. It is the first of the sciences through which the soul ascends from the senses to the intelligibles. The number one is the root of all numbers and is not itself a number, just as the Creator is the origin of all existents and is not Himself one of them.

translator_notes.note: The analogy between the number one and God reveals the Neopythagorean foundations of the Brethren's thought. Their mathematical mysticism parallels both the Pythagorean tradition and Jewish Kabbalistic numerology. The identity of the Brethren remains debated; they may have been Ismaili Shi'a intellectuals.
